deviations.gdpr_quote: High-risk AI systems shall comply with the requirements laid down in this Section, taking into account their intended purpose as well as the generally acknowledged state of the art on AI and AI-related technologies. The risk management system referred to in Article 9 shall be taken into account when ensuring compliance with those requirements.
deviations.mismatch: The regulation addresses compliance obligations for high-risk AI systems, while the policy extends these review/oversight obligations to all AI systems.
deviations.analysis: Applying the requirements to all AI systems is stricter than the regulation’s scope that targets high-risk AI systems; this is over-compliant.

deviations.gdpr_quote: The risk management system shall be understood as a continuous iterative process planned and run throughout the entire lifecycle of a high-risk AI system, requiring regular systematic review and updating.
deviations.policy_quote: Review your systems at least annually against the definitions for Sensitive Uses and Restricted Uses.
deviations.mismatch: The regulation requires a continuous, lifecycle-long iterative process with regular systematic review, whereas the policy sets a minimum annual review cadence.
deviations.analysis: An “at least annually” review cadence is weaker than a continuous iterative process across the lifecycle; this narrows the temporal rigor required by Article 9 as referenced by Article 8(1).

deviations.gdpr_quote: High-risk AI systems shall comply with the requirements laid down in this Section, taking into account their intended purpose as well as the generally acknowledged state of the art on AI and AI-related technologies. The risk management system referred to in Article 9 shall be taken into account when ensuring compliance with those requirements.
deviations.mismatch: The policy extends applicability beyond high-risk AI systems, whereas the regulation targets high-risk AI systems.
deviations.analysis: Art8(1) applies to high-risk systems and integrates Article 9’s risk management system (REG-003 to REG-012). The policy broadened scope to all AI systems, which is stricter than required (over-compliance). Other elements (state of the art, explicit risk management steps) are not contradicted but simply not present in this chunk; per rules, omissions are Non-Deviation.

deviations.gdpr_quote: The risk management system shall be understood as a continuous iterative process planned and run throughout the entire lifecycle of a high-risk AI system
deviations.mismatch: Article 9(2) limits the obligation to high-risk AI systems, whereas the policy requires assessments for all AI systems, which is stricter than required.
deviations.analysis: The regulation’s obligations apply to high-risk AI systems only; the policy’s blanket requirement for all AI systems represents an expansion in scope (over-compliance) without contradicting the regulation.

deviations.gdpr_quote: The risk management measures referred to in paragraph 2, point (d), shall give due consideration to the effects and possible interaction resulting from the combined application of the requirements set out in this Section, with a view to minimising risks more effectively while achieving an appropriate balance in implementing the measures to fulfil those requirements. ... The risk management system shall be understood as a continuous iterative process planned and run throughout the entire lifecycle of a high-risk AI system
deviations.mismatch: Article 9(4), via Art 9(2)(d), concerns measures for high-risk AI systems, while the policy applies the assessment requirement to all AI systems, which is stricter than required.
deviations.analysis: Paragraph 4’s obligations attach to the risk management system under 9(2)(d) for high-risk AI systems; extending equivalent assessment obligations to all AI systems expands the regulatory scope and constitutes over-compliance.

deviations.gdpr_quote: High-risk AI systems shall be tested for the purpose of identifying the most appropriate and targeted risk management measures. Testing shall ensure that high-risk AI systems perform consistently for their intended purpose and that they are in compliance with the requirements set out in this Section.
deviations.mismatch: The policy’s applicability is broader than the regulation’s, which requires testing duties specifically for high-risk AI systems.
deviations.analysis: Art9(6) testing duties target high-risk systems, further elaborated by Art9(7)–(10) (e.g., real-world testing, prior-defined metrics). The policy extends scope to all AI systems—over-compliance. No conflicting statements about testing methods or metrics; the remainder are omissions and thus Non-Deviation.

deviations.gdpr_quote: Testing shall ensure that high-risk AI systems perform consistently for their intended purpose and that they are in compliance with the requirements set out in this Section.
deviations.policy_quote: If there are Responsible Release Criteria for metrics or rubrics that have not been met, consult with the reviewers named in the Impact Assessment, and in the case of Sensitive Uses, with the Office of Responsible AI, to develop a plan detailing how the gap will be managed until it can be closed. Document that plan.
deviations.mismatch: The regulation requires testing to ensure compliance, whereas the policy contemplates managing unmet criteria via a gap-management plan rather than ensuring compliance through testing.
deviations.analysis: By allowing a managed plan for unmet criteria, the policy’s execution approach does not guarantee that testing ensures compliance as the Article requires.

deviations.gdpr_quote: High-risk AI systems which make use of techniques involving the training of AI models with data shall be developed on the basis of training, validation and testing data sets that meet the quality criteria referred to in paragraphs 2 to 5 whenever such data sets are used.
deviations.mismatch: The policy expands applicability from only high-risk AI systems to all AI systems.
deviations.analysis: Art10(1) applies specifically to high-risk AI systems; the policy imposes its requirement set on all AI systems, which is stricter than required. Art10(2) (REG-014) was reviewed; omissions about its details in the policy are treated as Non-Deviation.

deviations.gdpr_quote: High-risk AI systems which make use of techniques involving the training of AI models with data shall be developed on the basis of training, validation and testing data sets that meet the quality criteria referred to in paragraphs 2 to 5 whenever such data sets are used.
deviations.mismatch: The regulation limits the requirement to high-risk AI systems, whereas the policy applies it to all AI systems (over-compliance in scope).
deviations.analysis: Article 10(1) scopes obligations to high-risk AI systems; the policy extends these governance requirements to all AI systems, which is stricter than required.

deviations.gdpr_quote: Training, validation and testing data sets shall be subject to data governance and management practices appropriate for the intended purpose of the high-risk AI system. Those practices shall concern in particular: (a) the relevant design choices; (b) data collection processes and the origin of data, and in the case of personal data, the original purpose of the data collection; (c) relevant data-preparation processing operations, such as annotation, labelling, cleaning, updating, enrichment and aggregation; (d) the formulation of assumptions, in particular with respect to the information that the data are supposed to measure and represent; (e) an assessment of the availability, quantity and suitability of the data sets that are needed; (f) examination in view of possible biases that are likely to affect the health and safety of persons, have a negative impact on fundamental rights or lead to discrimination prohibited under Union law, especially where data outputs influence inputs for future operations; (g) appropriate measures to detect, prevent and mitigate possible biases identified according to point (f); (h) the identification of relevant data gaps or shortcomings that prevent compliance with this Regulation, and how those gaps and shortcomings can be addressed.
deviations.policy_quote: If you plan to use existing data sets to train the system, assess the quantity and suitability of available data sets that will be needed by the system in relation to the data requirements defined in A4.1. A4.3 Document this assessment in the Impact Assessment.
deviations.mismatch: The regulation requires assessing availability, quantity and suitability of all needed data sets (covering training, validation and testing), but the policy limits this assessment to cases using existing datasets to train the system.
deviations.analysis: Art 10(2) applies governance practices, including the (e) assessment, to training, validation and testing data sets that are needed; the policy narrows this to existing data sets and to the training phase, thereby restricting the data categories and processing stages covered by the assessment.

deviations.gdpr_quote: Training, validation and testing data sets shall be subject to data governance and management practices appropriate for the intended purpose of the high-risk AI system.
deviations.mismatch: The regulation targets high-risk AI systems, while the policy applies these practices to all AI systems.
deviations.analysis: The policy broadens the scope beyond the regulation’s high-risk AI system limitation, constituting over-compliance in scope.

deviations.gdpr_quote: including, where applicable, as regards the persons or groups of persons in relation to whom the high-risk AI system is intended to be used.
deviations.mismatch: The regulation’s dataset quality/statistical property requirements are framed in relation to high-risk AI systems, while the policy applies them to all AI systems.
deviations.analysis: By extending requirements to all AI systems, the policy is stricter in scope than the regulation specifies.

deviations.gdpr_quote: within which the high-risk AI system is intended to be used.
deviations.mismatch: The regulation’s requirement to account for contextual characteristics applies to high-risk AI systems, while the policy applies it to all AI systems.
deviations.analysis: The policy imposes this consideration across all AI systems rather than limiting to high-risk, indicating over-compliance.

deviations.gdpr_quote: For the development of high-risk AI systems not using techniques involving the training of AI models, paragraphs 2 to 5 apply only to the testing data sets.
deviations.mismatch: The policy expands applicability from only high-risk AI systems to all AI systems.
deviations.analysis: Art10(6) limits obligations to high-risk AI systems and, in that case, only to testing datasets; the policy’s blanket scope covers all AI systems, which is stricter. Art10(2) (REG-014) was considered; lack of explicit treatment in the policy is an omission (Non-Deviation).

deviations.gdpr_quote: High-risk AI systems shall be designed and developed in such a way as to ensure that their operation is sufficiently transparent to enable deployers to interpret a system's output and use it appropriately.
deviations.mismatch: The policy applies transparency/oversight expectations to all AI systems, while Article 13(1) is scoped to high-risk AI systems.
deviations.analysis: This is a stricter scope than required by the regulation; not a contradiction, but an over-compliant policy stance.

deviations.gdpr_quote: High-risk AI systems shall be designed and developed in such a way as to ensure that their operation is sufficiently transparent to enable deployers to interpret a system's output and use it appropriately.
deviations.policy_quote: Stakeholders must be able to understand: 3) how to interpret system behavior and the specific logic of the internal processing steps that led to the output
deviations.mismatch: The policy requires understanding the specific internal processing logic, exceeding the regulation’s requirement of transparency sufficient to interpret outputs.
deviations.analysis: Art 13(1) centers on output interpretability; mandating insight into specific internal processing logic raises the transparency bar beyond what the provision requires.

deviations.gdpr_quote: The instructions for use shall contain at least the following information: (a) the identity and the contact details of the provider and, where applicable, of its authorised representative; (b) the characteristics, capabilities and limitations of performance of the high-risk AI system, including: (i) its intended purpose;
deviations.policy_quote: 1) remove the intended use from customer-facing materials and make current customers aware of the issue, take action to close the identified gap, or discontinue the system,
deviations.mismatch: While the regulation requires that instructions for use include the intended purpose, the policy directs removing the intended use from customer-facing materials when evidence is lacking.
deviations.analysis: Customer-facing materials typically include instructions for use; authorizing removal of the intended use results in documentation that omits the required intended purpose, contradicting the mandate that it shall be included.

deviations.gdpr_quote: (vi) when appropriate, specifications for the input data, or any other relevant information in terms of the training, validation and testing data sets used, taking into account the intended purpose of the high-risk AI system;
deviations.mismatch: The regulation targets only high-risk AI systems, whereas the policy applies the same measures to all AI systems (over-compliance).
deviations.analysis: The policy broadens the scope of documentation requirements from high-risk AI systems to all AI systems, making it stricter than required.

deviations.gdpr_quote: (vii) where applicable, information to enable deployers to interpret the output of the high-risk AI system and use it appropriately; ... (d) the human oversight measures referred to in Article 14, including the technical measures put in place to facilitate the interpretation of the outputs of the high-risk AI systems by the deployers; (e) the computational and hardware resources needed, the expected lifetime of the high-risk AI system and any necessary maintenance and care measures, including their frequency, to ensure the proper functioning of that AI system, including as regards software updates; (f) where relevant, a description of the mechanisms included within the high-risk AI system that allows deployers to properly collect, store and interpret the logs in accordance with Article 12.
deviations.mismatch: The policy applies the Art 13(8) style information and oversight support to all AI systems, whereas the regulation targets high-risk AI systems.
deviations.analysis: This is an over-compliant expansion of scope. References to Art 14 and Art 12 in Art 13(8) do not change its high-risk scope. Omissions about resources, lifetime, maintenance, and logs are Non-Deviation.

deviations.gdpr_quote: (vii) where applicable, information to enable deployers to interpret the output of the high-risk AI system and use it appropriately; ... (d) the human oversight measures referred to in Article 14, including the technical measures put in place to facilitate the interpretation of the outputs of the high-risk AI systems by the deployers;
deviations.policy_quote: Stakeholders must be able to understand: 3) how to interpret system behavior and the specific logic of the internal processing steps that led to the output
deviations.mismatch: The policy requires understanding the specific internal processing logic leading to outputs, which is stricter than the regulation’s requirement to provide information and measures enabling interpretation of outputs.
deviations.analysis: Art 13(8) focuses on ensuring interpretability of outputs; mandating comprehension of the system’s specific internal logic imposes a higher transparency burden than required.

deviations.gdpr_quote: High-risk AI systems shall be designed and developed in such a way, including with appropriate human-machine interface tools, that they can be effectively overseen by natural persons during the period in which they are in use.
deviations.mismatch: The policy applies oversight-related design provisions to all AI systems, whereas Art 14(1) applies only to high-risk AI systems.
deviations.analysis: This is a scope alteration from ‘high-risk AI systems’ to ‘all AI systems’; no contradiction, but the category/scope differs from the regulation.

deviations.gdpr_quote: Human oversight shall aim to prevent or minimise the risks to health, safety or fundamental rights that may emerge when a high-risk AI system is used in accordance with its intended purpose or under conditions of reasonably foreseeable misuse, in particular where such risks persist despite the application of other requirements set out in this Section.
deviations.policy_quote: Microsoft AI systems are reviewed to identify systems that may have a significant adverse impact on people, organizations, and society, and additional oversight and requirements are applied to those systems.
deviations.mismatch: The regulation targets protection of health, safety or fundamental rights of persons, while the policy’s oversight trigger extends to impacts on organizations and society as well.
deviations.analysis: By broadening the protected subjects (organizations and society), the policy’s oversight scope exceeds the regulation’s focus on individuals’ health, safety, and fundamental rights; this is over-compliance.

deviations.gdpr_quote: Human oversight shall aim to prevent or minimise the risks to health, safety or fundamental rights that may emerge when a high-risk AI system is used in accordance with its intended purpose or under conditions of reasonably foreseeable misuse, in particular where such risks persist despite the application of other requirements set out in this Section.
deviations.mismatch: The policy applies oversight controls to all AI systems, while Article 14(2) addresses oversight for high-risk AI systems.
deviations.analysis: Scope is broadened beyond the high-risk AI systems referenced in Article 14; this is stricter than required. The policy otherwise aligns with elements from Art 14(4) (e.g., automation bias, override/interrupt), and omissions (e.g., explicit mention of risks/fundamental rights or foreseeable misuse) are treated as Non-Deviation.

deviations.gdpr_quote: The oversight measures shall be commensurate with the risks, level of autonomy and context of use of the high-risk AI system, and shall be ensured through either one or both of the following types of measures:
deviations.mismatch: The regulation’s oversight requirement targets high-risk AI systems, whereas the policy applies oversight to all AI systems, which is broader.
deviations.analysis: Policy scope exceeds the regulatory scope by applying oversight broadly beyond high-risk AI; this is over-compliance rather than a contradiction.

deviations.gdpr_quote: The oversight measures shall be commensurate with the risks, level of autonomy and context of use of the high-risk AI system, and shall be ensured through either one or both of the following types of measures: (a) measures identified and built, when technically feasible, into the high-risk AI system by the provider before it is placed on the market or put into service; (b) measures identified by the provider before placing the high-risk AI system on the market or putting it into service and that are appropriate to be implemented by the deployer.
deviations.policy_quote: When possible, design the system elements identified in A5.2. When this is not possible (for example, when Microsoft is not responsible for the system UX), provide guidance on human oversight considerations to the third party responsible for implementing the system elements identified in A5.2.
deviations.mismatch: The regulation uses technical feasibility as the criterion for built-in oversight measures, whereas the policy substitutes organizational possibility (e.g., responsibility for UX) as the determinant.
deviations.analysis: Art14(3)(a) hinges on 'when technically feasible' for provider-built measures; the policy’s 'when possible' example focuses on organizational responsibility (not being responsible for UX) to switch to guidance, changing the regulatory decision basis from technical feasibility to organizational constraints.

deviations.gdpr_quote: For the purpose of implementing paragraphs 1, 2 and 3, the high-risk AI system shall be provided to the deployer in such a way that natural persons to whom human oversight is assigned are enabled, as appropriate and proportionate:
deviations.mismatch: The policy applies the oversight enablement to all AI systems, whereas Art 14(4) applies specifically to high-risk AI systems.
deviations.analysis: Art 14(4) is scoped to high-risk AI systems and implements Art 14(1)–(3) (REG-033, REG-034, REG-035); the policy broadens the scope to all AI systems, altering the regulatory scope.

deviations.gdpr_quote: (c) to correctly interpret the high-risk AI system's output, taking into account, for example, the interpretation tools and methods available;
deviations.policy_quote: Stakeholders must be able to understand: 3) how to interpret system behavior and the specific logic of the internal processing steps that led to the output
deviations.mismatch: The policy requires understanding the specific internal processing logic, which is stricter than the regulation’s requirement to correctly interpret the system’s output using available tools.
deviations.analysis: Art 14(4)(c) focuses on correct interpretation of outputs (with available tools/methods), not necessarily the internal processing logic; requiring knowledge of specific internal steps imposes a stricter standard.

deviations.gdpr_quote: High-risk AI systems shall be designed and developed in such a way that they achieve an appropriate level of accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity, and that they perform consistently in those respects throughout their lifecycle.
deviations.mismatch: The policy applies the requirement to all AI systems, while the regulation addresses high-risk AI systems specifically.
deviations.analysis: The regulation limits the obligation to high-risk systems; the policy broadens scope to all AI systems, which is over-compliant in terms of applicability. No other contradictions identified; missing elements (accuracy/robustness/cybersecurity specifics) are treated as omissions and thus Non-Deviation.

deviations.gdpr_quote: Without prejudice to paragraphs 1 and 2, to the extent the deployer exercises control over the input data, that deployer shall ensure that input data is relevant and sufficiently representative in view of the intended purpose of the high-risk AI system.
deviations.mismatch: The policy expands applicability from only high-risk AI systems to all AI systems.
deviations.analysis: Art26(4) applies to high-risk AI systems and places an obligation on the deployer; the policy applies its requirements to all AI systems, which is stricter. Art26(1) (REG-043) and Art26(2) (REG-044) were considered; the policy’s lack of explicit deployer assignment is an omission, not a contradiction.

deviations.gdpr_quote: Without prejudice to paragraphs 1 and 2, to the extent the deployer exercises control over the input data, that deployer shall ensure that input data is relevant and sufficiently representative in view of the intended purpose of the high-risk AI system.
deviations.policy_quote: Microsoft AI systems are subject to appropriate data governance and management practices. Applies to: All AI systems.
deviations.mismatch: The regulation limits the obligation to deployers of high-risk AI systems, whereas the policy applies these governance/evaluation obligations to all AI systems, which is stricter in scope.
deviations.analysis: Art 26(4), read with 26(1)–(2), scopes obligations to deployers of high-risk AI systems; the policy explicitly applies to all AI systems, expanding the scope beyond the regulation. This is over-compliance rather than a contradiction.
